school: A local high school in the Cave Springs / Springdale area

questions.q: Do you see yourself going into a STEM field?
questions.a: I like science a lot, especially when we get to do experiments. But honestly, I don't really know what jobs are out there. Like, I know 'engineer' and 'doctor,' but what does an engineer actually do every day? Nobody around here does that.

questions.q: What has your experience been like with science classes here?
questions.a: We have good teachers, but we don't have a lot of equipment. Most of our labs are watching videos of experiments instead of doing them. It's hard to get excited about something you can only watch.

questions.q: If you could change one thing about STEM education here, what would it be?
questions.a: I'd want to meet people who actually work in science. Like, have them come talk to us or let us visit where they work. I think that would make it feel real instead of just something in a textbook.

school: A local high school in the Cave Springs / Springdale area

questions.q: What is the biggest challenge you face teaching STEM here?
questions.a: Funding, without question. I write grants constantly. Last year I got a small one for lab supplies, but it covered maybe two months of experiments. The rest of the year, we improvise.

questions.q: Do your students express interest in STEM careers?
questions.a: Some do, but there's a disconnect. They're interested in the ideas but can't picture themselves in those careers. They don't see representation—nobody in their family or community works in STEM. It's an abstract concept.
